topic_title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — giving AI access to external knowledge at query time

citation_density.detail: All three resources cited liberally throughout sections 5, 6, 7. Primary resource [1] cited on first factual claim (introduction, line 35) and consistently throughout all core sections. [2] and [3] cited in sections 5, 6, 7, 8, 10. Estimated citation coverage ~95% of standalone factual assertions — well above the 60% threshold. No [N] markers found that fail to resolve to a declared resource.

soft_findings.context: "The model's parameters do not change at all. Only the input changes."
soft_findings.finding: The term 'parameters' (model weights/internal numeric settings) is used without a plain-English gloss. It is not listed in concepts_introduced for 4.1 or 4.2 in the sequence index. However, it appears contextually in a contrast with fine-tuning (which was introduced in 4.2) and its meaning is implied by that contrast. Impact is low; no hard forward reference.

soft_findings.context: "connecting a general-purpose large language model (LLM) to a continuously updated article database"
soft_findings.finding: Acronym is spelled out on first use — 'large language model (LLM)'. The concept LLM is not listed in any prior topic's concepts_introduced in the sequence index (all weeks 1-3 have concepts_introduced: []); 4.1 and 4.2 use 'foundation model'. The term appears once in a real-world pattern example and is not load-bearing for understanding RAG. Low severity — acceptable as incidental reference given the spell-out. Recommend replacing with 'foundation model' for curriculum consistency.

sequence_drift.detail: No hard forward references to un-introduced concepts detected. All core vocabulary (knowledge cutoff, knowledge source, query, prompt augmentation, retrieve-augment-generate pipeline) is in 4.3's own concepts_introduced. Foundation model, adaptation, hallucination, fine-tuning, and related terms are covered in 4.1 and 4.2. The four Week-14 deferred terms (embeddings, vector databases, similarity search, chunking) are explicitly flagged as deferred in s5.2 and s5.5 — not treated as sequence violations.

length_sanity.detail: Corpus word count is estimated at ~2725 words, approximately 9% below the standard-depth corpus floor of 3000 words. The hard-fail threshold is 30% outside the range (floor of 2100 words); this corpus is well above that limit. No hard fail. Soft advisory: the corpus sits just below the standard floor — sections 7 (Real-World Patterns) or 8 (Best Practices) could absorb ~275 additional words on the next pass if depth is needed, but the existing content is substantive, well-structured, and grounded. The depth_profile shown in topic.manifest.json is 'standard'; the content complexity and depth signals (three-step pipeline, RAG-vs-fine-tuning comparison, multiple real-world patterns) are consistent with standard or deep classification.

topic_scope.cycle_1_failure_description: Section 5.5 previously contained four Week-14 concepts (embeddings, vector database, similarity search, chunking) bolded and followed by full defining clauses — teaching deferred concepts. Hard fail in cycle 1.
topic_scope.cycle_2_resolution: Section 5.5 (line 147-149) now contains a single sentence naming all four terms in italics (not bold) with an explicit Week-14 pointer and a 'you do not need to understand how any of these work at this stage' disclaimer. No defining clauses. No bold. This satisfies the bare-name + pointer form required by scope_discipline. Cycle-1 failure resolved.

scope_creep_findings.text: "...using a concept called similarity search over embeddings stored in a vector database — is a Week 14 topic."
scope_creep_findings.finding: The three deferred terms (similarity search, embeddings, vector database) appear in italics with an explicit Week-14 pointer — format is correct. However, the clause 'similarity search over embeddings stored in a vector database' reveals the architectural relationship between all three terms (embeddings are stored in a vector database; similarity search operates over them). This is marginally more than a bare name: it encodes one piece of Week-14 architecture in passing. Strict reading of scope_discipline ('bare name plus the topic pointer — never followed by a defining clause or appositive') flags this.
scope_creep_findings.recommendation: Simplify to: '(The technical mechanism — how the retrieval step finds relevant passages — involves concepts called embeddings, vector databases, and similarity search; these are covered in Week 14.)' This removes the relational framing while preserving the deferral intent.

topic_scope.detail: Section 5.5 is clean. The only remaining scope finding is a soft one in Section 5.2 where deferred terms are framed relationally in a parenthetical. Does not constitute teaching and does not gate a hard fail. Topic headline scope (RAG conceptual introduction: problem, three-step pipeline, comparison with fine-tuning, real-world use cases) is respected throughout. No section teaches beyond the headline.

findings.detail: The term 'parameters' is used without a plain-English gloss on first use: 'The model's parameters do not change at all.' A brief inline definition — e.g. 'the model's internal numeric settings (its parameters) do not change at all' — would satisfy the define-on-first-use mandate for a zero-background learner. The term recurs in the comparison table (section 5.4) and Key Takeaways without definition.

Acronym is spelled out ('large language model (LLM)'). Low severity — single appearance, not relied upon. Recommend replacing with 'foundation model' for consistency with the rest of the curriculum and to avoid introducing an unexplained synonym.

beginner_readability.detail: One distinct soft finding type (undefined_terms). Well below the 3-distinct-finding escalation threshold. No jargon dumps detected — the four Week-14 terms in s5.5 are presented as a single named list (not a dump) with explicit deferral. No hedging phrases. Sentence length and register are appropriate for a zero-background fresher audience. The open-book vs closed-book exam analogy (Introduction) and the colleague-reads-policy analogy (Section 5.3) are well-calibrated for the target audience. Formatting is consistent with guidelines: bold terms on definition, numbered list for ordered steps, comparison table in section 5.4, inline ASCII pipeline diagram in section 5.2.

soft_findings: length_sanity: corpus ~9% below standard-depth 3000-word floor (estimated 2725w); within 30% hard-fail band
sequence_drift: 'parameters' used without gloss (line 59); 'LLM' used once (line 174) — not in sequence concepts_introduced
topic_scope: s5.2 line 71 — deferred terms in relational clause rather than bare-name form; soft only
beginner_readability: 'parameters' undefined on first use; 'LLM' should be replaced with 'foundation model' for curriculum consistency
summary.action_required: None required to proceed — all hard checks pass. Optional improvements on next authoring pass: (1) simplify s5.2 line 71 to remove relational framing from deferred-term clause; (2) add inline gloss for 'parameters' on first use; (3) replace 'LLM' with 'foundation model' in section 7.

verdict: PASS. All six hard checks clear. Cycle-1 scope_creep failure on Section 5.5 is resolved: the revised section names embeddings, vector databases, similarity search, and chunking as bare italicised terms with a single explicit Week-14 pointer — no bold, no defining clauses, no relational architecture framing. Four soft findings recorded (none gate the pass): corpus is ~9% below the 3000-word standard floor; 'parameters' undefined on first use; 'LLM' appears once without prior sequence introduction; s5.2 deferred-term clause has mild relational framing. Corpus is cleared to proceed to artifact fan-out (reading + quiz + diagram).
